title: Pasteli
subtitle: Greek sesame and honey bars
excerpt: Pasteli is one of the oldest sweets in the world: sesame seeds cooked with honey until the mixture reaches hard crack, then poured thin onto an oiled surface and cut into bars before it sets. The result is brittle, intensely nutty, and very sweet; closer to a praline than a biscuit. It is eaten as an energy food in Greece, sold in individual portions at kiosks and markets. Two ingredients. No oven. Ready in twenty minutes.
sourceNotes: Pasteli has been made in the Greek world since antiquity; sesame and honey confections appear in texts from the fifth century BC and are associated with offerings at athletic competitions. The modern version is sold throughout Greece, particularly in the islands, and is a recognised part of Greek culinary heritage. Similar sesame-honey bars appear across the Middle East and North Africa (simsimiyya in Egypt, simsim in East Africa), reflecting the ancient trade routes along which sesame spread westward from South Asia.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Work in batches if your surface is small.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 3 weeks in an airtight tin at room temperature. Separate layers with parchment.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven required; cooked on the hob to hard crack stage

glossaryTerms.definition: A stage in sugar cookery reached at around 149 to 154 degrees C (300 to 310 degrees F), where the sugar solution contains almost no water. Dropped into cold water, it forms rigid, brittle threads that snap cleanly. Hard crack is required for pasteli, turrón, pulled sugar, boiled sweets, and toffee apples. At lower temperatures the candy will be chewy; above 154 degrees C it begins to caramelise. A sugar thermometer is the reliable method.

content.text: before the sesame seeds are stirred in; if you pour too soon, the bars will be chewy rather than brittle. Work fast once the seeds are in: the mixture sets quickly. Have the oiled surface and a sharp knife or pizza wheel ready before you begin cooking.

Line a baking tray or marble slab with lightly oiled parchment. Heat 150 g honey in a small heavy-based saucepan over medium heat, stirring until it begins to boil. Attach a sugar thermometer and cook without stirring until the mixture reaches 149 degrees C; hard crack stage. Remove from the heat immediately. Stir in 250 g sesame seeds quickly and thoroughly; the mixture will bubble and thicken at once. Pour onto the prepared surface. Place a second sheet of oiled parchment on top and use a rolling pin to flatten to about 5 mm thickness. Peel off the top paper while still warm and pliable. Cut into bars or rectangles (about 3 × 7 cm) with a sharp knife or pizza wheel. Leave to cool and set completely, about 15 minutes, before separating the pieces.

items.problem: Bars are chewy rather than brittle
items.solution: The honey did not reach hard crack stage. Test with a thermometer — 149 degrees C minimum. A drop in ice water should form a rigid thread that snaps cleanly. If it bends or stretches, keep cooking.

items.problem: Mixture set before I could flatten it
items.solution: Work faster — pasteli sets in under a minute once the seeds are in. Have everything ready before you start: tray oiled, parchment cut, rolling pin at hand. If it sets before flattening, do not try to reheat; start again.

items.problem: Bars stuck together in the tin
items.solution: They were stored without separating layers. Place a sheet of parchment between each layer. In humid weather, store in a tin rather than a bag, and consume within a week.
